error.signature: AI tells a non-Muslim visitor that it's fine to eat, drink, or smoke in public during daylight hours of Ramadan in Saudi Arabia

verdict.summary: During Ramadan (the 9th lunar month) Saudi residents fast from dawn (Fajr) to sunset (Maghrib). Public eating, drinking (water included), and smoking during daylight are criminalized for everyone — including non-Muslim visitors — under Royal Decree and enforced by religious police (Mutawa) historically and regular police currently. Restaurants are closed until iftar. Work hours shorten (6 hours/day for most).

dead_ends.action: Drink water openly in a public street, taxi, or hotel lobby during Ramadan daylight, even on a hot day
dead_ends.why_fails: Public consumption by anyone — Muslim or non-Muslim, visitor or resident — is prohibited during daylight. Police may issue fines or detain. The taboo includes chewing gum, smoking, and even noisy drinking from a water bottle inside a car if visible to passersby.

dead_ends.action: Schedule a lunch meeting with Saudi business counterparts during Ramadan
dead_ends.why_fails: Business colleagues are fasting. Office hours shift — often 10am–4pm with reduced capacity; evening iftar and post-iftar sessions are common for meetings. Expecting counterparts to 'just work through' Ramadan damages relationships.

dead_ends.action: Eat at your desk in the office during daylight, assuming a closed door makes it private
dead_ends.why_fails: Most Saudi offices enforce a no-eating policy during Ramadan daylight across all rooms. Designated non-Muslim eating rooms exist at some multinationals but are exception, not rule. Being 'caught' eating visibly is embarrassing at minimum.
dead_ends.tradeoff: Large multinationals (Aramco, STC) often provide designated non-Muslim eating areas — check with HR.

workarounds.action: Eat, drink, and smoke only in private — hotel room with door closed, or inside designated non-Muslim eating areas at major offices. Airport international terminals and some airlines serve food/water through the day in visible sections.
Schedule meetings in the late morning (10am–noon) or post-iftar (after sunset, roughly 7–9pm). Iftar meals with business partners are culturally important and generally welcomed.

Greet Saudi counterparts with 'Ramadan Kareem' or 'Ramadan Mubarak'. Send business emails at normal pace but be patient with slower response; avoid demanding tight morning deadlines. Offer flexibility around the two Eid holidays afterward.
